turns.content: I'm looking at the tutorial for this new string manipulation crate but I don't understand what splitn does compared to just using split, why would anyone use that specific method instead of the more general one?

The key distinction is that splitn returns an iterator with a fixed count rather than consuming the entire string like standard split. For example if you have a comma separated list and want exactly three parts you call splitn(3) which yields three strings instead of continuing to yield until empty. This matters when you know how many tokens you expect upfront.
But I thought split would just stop if it ran out of characters so why am I being told to use splitn for this exact scenario where the input is always three items?

You're right that split handles variable length inputs but splitn guarantees exactly n outputs without error if you expect fewer. Can you tell me what specific error message or behavior you saw when you tried using split on a fixed three item list?
I'm trying to migrate this legacy C project over, and I thought the Rust way of handling strings was just a fancy version of split_whitespace with an extra argument.

That's a reasonable guess given how similar C's strtok is to Rust's split methods. The key difference isn't in the number of arguments, but in what you're actually passing into the function. When you call split_whitespace on a String, it returns an iterator over owned Strings because that's how the String type works internally with iterators. If you want borrowed slices instead, you need to use &str and pass a reference like &input.split_whitespace(). This changes everything because you can't return owned strings from a function that only borrows input without cloning or reallocating memory.
Ah okay so I should just be using split_once if I only need to separate the first token. But what about when I need to join them back together? Does that require a specific trait?

You're right, split_once is useful for exactly that scenario where you just want to grab one delimiter and keep the rest intact. For joining, you don't need any special traits beyond the standard iterator adapter. You can collect the iterator into a Vec<String> using collect::<Vec<String>>(), then use join(&mut vec) or more commonly in modern Rust with iter().collect::<String>(). This avoids manual allocation because String implements FromIterator which handles buffering efficiently without needing to manually manage a buffer yourself.

I'm trying to parse a configuration file where each line contains key-value pairs separated by colons, but I keep getting confused about how to handle multiple delimiters. Should I use split or is there a better way?

You are likely overthinking this if you are looking for a single method that does everything at once. In Rust, the standard approach is to iterate through lines and apply split on each line individually using the colon delimiter. You would call split(':') on every string from your configuration file, which returns an iterator of Vec<String> results. This avoids loading massive strings into memory because it processes one line at a time.
Okay, so I iterate over lines and split each one by colon to get the key and value? But what if there are multiple colons on a single line like 'host:port:name'?

That is exactly why you should use split_once instead of split in that scenario. While split splits at every occurrence, which would give you three separate parts for your example string, split_once only splits on the first delimiter found and returns a tuple containing the part before it and everything after it. This preserves the rest of the data as a single substring, allowing you to handle multiple colons in one value field without losing information.
